title: SNEAKO interviews Professor Jiang: Global Crisis Incoming

read.title: When War Becomes a Script and Fame Becomes a Trap
read.subtitle: Sneako interviews Professor Jiang after the Iran war predictions go viral
read.voice_policy: Direct argumentative prose derived from the interview. Preserve Sneako's pressure where it shapes Jiang's answers, while keeping disputed claims as dated source claims rather than external verification.
read.trace_policy: Paragraph-level transcript refs with selective evidence marks on sharp thesis phrases, dated predictions, and jump-worthy moments.
read.dek: Sneako presses Jiang after the Iran war turns him into a sudden internet figure. Jiang answers with a warning: war does not merely kill abroad. It scripts civil discord, rewards religious extremism, turns media personalities into controlled assets, and tempts the teacher to become a player in the very game he is trying to explain.
read.thesis: The interview is not just about whether Jiang predicted the Iran war. It is about what prediction does to the person who becomes useful to the crowd. Jiang reads the war as an eschatological and imperial script: Christian Zionism, Al-Aqsa, the Third Temple, Trump's survival politics, draft pressure, Gulf fragility, and Greater Israel all move through the same logic of escalation. But he also turns the lens back on himself and on Sneako: fame is a drug, monetization is an invitation to control, education is self-annihilation of brainwashing, and the best survival asset is not crypto or platform reach but human relationships.

opening.heading: Core Reading
opening.text: The strange force of this interview is that the war and the influencer economy become one problem. Sneako wants to know why the Iran war is escalating, why anti-war voices are being attacked, why protests are absent, and why people who should agree are turning on each other. Jiang keeps answering at two scales at once. At the geopolitical scale, war is a script that needs civil discord, religious acceleration, and imperial overreach. At the personal scale, sudden fame is also a script: it offers money, status, enemies, fans, and a role to play. Jiang's answer is to keep the teacher separate from the player.

In the middle of a war, the question of whether he is a credentialed professor is a distraction.

beats.heading: War Makes People Crazy
beats.summary: Sneako asks whether escalation can still be avoided; Jiang says a deal is possible in theory, but war produces its own adrenaline and loss-of-face logic.

paragraphs.text: Sneako pushes the immediate strategic question: why not negotiate with Iran, reopen Hormuz, and avoid ground invasion? Jiang says the deal exists. If the United States stopped the war, closed Middle East bases, and offered a win-win arrangement around navigation, investment, and dollar-system participation, Iran would likely negotiate. This is the absurdity: the art of the deal is available, but the war path is chosen.

beats.heading: Eschatology As Political Engine
beats.summary: The Christian Zionism and Al-Aqsa discussion turns religious belief into a material political engine: money, lobbying, red heifers, temple preparation, and crisis timing.

paragraphs.text: Asked about evangelical rhetoric, Jiang returns to an older prediction: a U.S. invasion of Iran would be driven by Christian Zionism, Saudi Arabia, and imperial hubris. He emphasizes the first more than many viewers expect. For Christian Zionists, Middle East war is not simply policy. It is a way to trigger prophecy: war, Al-Aqsa, Third Temple, Jewish return, Gog and Magog, Second Coming.

beats.heading: Social Media As Civil-War Training
beats.summary: Instead of censorship, Jiang suspects selective amplification: let anti-war voices speak, then turn allies into enemies and despair into civil discord.

paragraphs.text: Jiang compares this moment to COVID and sees an inversion. During COVID, dissent was censored. In this war, prominent anti-war figures are amplified. That does not reassure him. If the war is meant to produce civil discord, then letting people speak can also be a way to make them fight, split, and despair.

The trick worked so well that young people became hard to reason with, but it also created a new problem: the same moral machinery could turn against Gaza.

beats.heading: Trump As Projection And Survival Machine
beats.summary: Jiang reads Trump through managed succession, projection, third-term survival, and the military optics culture that turns war into spectacle.

paragraphs.text: Jiang’s Clinton-Bush story is a model of political succession as managed transformation. He argues that Clinton could do what Bush could not: pass NAFTA, open China trade, and turn the Democratic Party from a working-class party into the coastal professional elite party. That frame prepares the Trump claim: Biden could not launch the Iran war without MAGA revolt, but Trump can.

Jiang treats that as fantasy, but fantasy with political force.

paragraphs.text: The third-term prediction follows from survival. Jiang says Trump will not leave office because leaving means Democratic prosecution of him and his family. The details are uncertain; the survival incentive is the structure.

beats.heading: Greater Israel And The Cost Of Redemption
beats.summary: The conversation moves from draft pressure and sabotage into Kabbalah, Greater Israel, Gulf collapse, and the claim that sacred redemption is being pursued through regional destruction.

paragraphs.text: Jiang says war has a logical momentum of its own: if the United States starts bombing Iran, it then needs ground forces to solve the air-defense problems produced by the bombing. Sneako names this as sunken cost. Jiang extends it to draft pressure, rationing, National Guard control, German draft movement, low morale, and even fragging or sabotage if young men are sent to die in the Middle East.

Survival capital is trust.
